About: It is about a cross and circle board game.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the ancient text Mahabharata.
ConsistsOf: It consists of pieces and cowrie shells.
CreatedIn: It was created in Ancient India.
HasA: It has a board shaped like a symmetrical cross.
It has pieces that move around the board.
It has cowrie shells used as lots.
It has a name derived from the Hindi word paccīs.
It has other versions with different largest scores.
It has similar games that originated around the world.
It has a history identified from the Iron Age.
It has cruciform boards depicted from art reliefs.
It has a board identical to pachisi discovered in the Ellora cave system.
It has a mass market board game version called Game of India.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by a cross and circle board.
It is characterized by pieces moving based upon a throw of cowrie shells.
It is characterized by a name meaning 'twenty-five'.
It is characterized by similar games originating around the world.
It is characterized by historical identification from the Iron Age.
It is characterized by cruciform boards depicted in art reliefs.
It is characterized by a board discovered in the Ellora cave system.
It is characterized by a regal manner of play by Akbar.
It is characterized by a game for two, three, or four players.
It is characterized by beehive-shaped pieces.
IsA: It is a cross and circle board game.
It is a game played on a board shaped like a symmetrical cross.
It is a game also known by the name Twenty-Five.
It is a game with similar versions around the world.
It is a game identified from the Iron Age.
It is a game played by Akbar.
It is a game for two, three, or four players.
It is a game with beehive-shaped pieces.
It is a game played counter-clockwise.
It is a game where pieces can be captured.
KnownAs: It is known as Parchís in Spain and northern Morocco.
It is known as Parqués in Colombia.
It is known as Parcheesi, Patchesi, Sorry!, and Ludo in commercial versions.
It is known as jeu des petits chevaux in France.
It is known as Mensch ärgere Dich nicht in Germany.
KnownFor: It is known for its origin in Ancient India.
It is known for its name meaning 'twenty-five'.
It is known for its cross and circle board.
It is known for its use of cowrie shells.
It is known for its historical significance.
It is known for its association with Emperor Akbar.
It is known for its variations and similar games worldwide.
NamedAfter: It is named after the Hindi word paccīs, meaning 'twenty-five'.
PerformedBy: It was performed by Emperor Akbar and his courtiers.
PublishedAs: It was published as Game of India by Transogram.
It was published as Pa-Chiz-Si: The Game of India by Transogram.
UsedAs: It is used as a game.
It is used as a basis for similar games worldwide.
It is used as a historical artifact.
It is used as a form of entertainment.
It is used as a gambling game in the Mahabharata.
It is used as a royal pastime by Emperor Akbar.
It is used as a commercial board game version.
UsedFor: It is used for playing a cross and circle board game.
It is used for moving pieces around a board.
It is used for determining movement based on throws.
It is used for royal pastimes.
It is used for commercial versions of similar games.
UsedIn: It is used in Ancient India.
It is used in the ancient text Mahabharata.
It is used in games like Barjis in the Levant.
It is used in Parchís in Spain and northern Morocco.
It is used in Parqués in Colombia.
It is used in commercial versions like Parcheesi, Patchesi, Sorry!, and Ludo.
It is used in jeu des petits chevaux in France.
It is used in Mensch ärgere Dich nicht in Germany.
It is used in historical findings from the Iron Age.
It is used in art reliefs from Chandraketugarh.
UsedTo: It is used to move pieces around the board.
It is used to determine the number of spaces to move.
It is used to introduce pieces onto the board.
It is used to capture opponent's pieces.
It is used to return pieces to the starting position.
It is used to nullify higher value throws.
It is used to keep a turn going.
